posts.text: Thinking out loud: wonder if this also has implications for DiLoCo/local-SGD in heavily overtrained regimes. If late-training weight fluctuation envelopes shrink, could between-sync replica drift or the outer update become large relative to that envelope and hurt vs baseline?…

Was briefly reading on scaling laws for quantisation. Counterintuitively, more pretraining tokens worsens the PTQ degradation. Two papers observed this empirically (Kumar et al. (https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.04330) and Chen et al. (https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14302)) but neither explained why.

6/ Our paper can be found here: https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.03597. We welcome feedbacks and comments on our work.

5/ Beyond inference, NuMuon potentially unlocks a more communication-friendly optimizer suited for distributed training. Our updates are inherently low-rank, so we can potentially communicate smaller components during training — a direct path toward more communication-friendly…

4/ Across 48+ configurations, NuMuon consistently beats Muon and AdamW at high compression rates when using SOTA LLM compression methods. This means: at the same model quality, NuMuon lets you compress more which means a smaller model and hence, faster inference at serving time!

3/ To fix this issue, we built NuMuon: Muon + a nuclear norm constraint on the updates to control the weight space rank. This way, instead of full-rank updates, NuMuon uses top-K SVD updates. We theoretically show that NuMuon holds similar convergence properties to Muon, but the…

2/ We surprisingly find that despite having full-rank updates, Muon-trained models have low-rank weights! We confirmed this across our own trained models as well as publicly available ones like Moonlight-16B and Kimi-K2. This means that Muon-trained models are still compressible;…

1/ Muon has become a popular optimizer of choice for training large language models (LLMs). But there's an open question: since Muon updates are full-rank by design, does this make Muon-trained models hard to compress? This matters for both fast inference and communication…

In this new era powered by autonomous agents, we have to reinvent ourselves and try to adapt. Experiments that could take days to design and run can now run much faster, making iteration easier. Great work led by @tha_ajanthan on async LM training using data/pipeline parallelism. The key idea is to use weight look-ahead to handle pipeline staleness, and EMA-corrected sparse averaging to handle data parallel staleness. No expensive sync needed. https://x.com/tha_ajanthan/status/2024649548522803638

It's disappointing to see many papers submitted to big confs lately lack novel/interesting points. I started reviewing in 2020 with the goal of learning sth new from each paper. That's become very rare. I wonder what would happen if there is no pressure to publish.

#CVPR

Please find our full paper or visit our repository to reproduce the results:
[Paper]: https://openreview.net/forum?id=H2aMtyFhQh
[Code]: https://github.com/hmdolatabadi/AVATAR

(5/5)

This work was conducted during my postdoctoral fellowship at the University of Melbourne, in collaboration with Sarah Erfani and Christopher Leckie. We gratefully acknowledge the support of ARC Center of Excellence for Automated Decision-Making and Society (@AdmsCentre).

(4/5)

Our proposed AVATAR framework successfully reconstructs perturbed data. These findings highlight the limitations of current unlearnable example techniques and underscore the need for more robust privacy methods in the age of #generative_AI.

(3/5)

This paper challenges the notion of "unlearnable examples" as a data privacy solution. We demonstrate that imperceptible perturbations intended to protect personal data can be reversed by an adversary using diffusion models.

(2/5)

I'm pleased to announce that my research paper, "The Devil's Advocate: Shattering the Illusion of Unexploitable Data using Diffusion Models," has been accepted at the IEEE Conference on Secure and Trustworthy Machine Learning (@satml_conf).

(1/5)
For those attending @ICCVConference: Musée d'Orsay has a nice Van Gogh exhibition in case anyone is interested!

For more information, please refer to:

arXiv: https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.05785
GitHub: https://github.com/hmdolatabadi/ACS

(5/n, n=5)

... we can ensure that the solution derived for robust training gives a good approximation of the optimal solution derived using the complete dataset.

Please read through our paper if you are interested. Our implementation has also been made online.

(4/n)

We conduct a detailed theoretical analysis of adversarial coreset selection. Succinctly put, we establish that subject to certain regularity constraints, by harnessing a weighted subset's capacity to estimate the loss gradient across the complete dataset...

(3/n)

Building upon the foundation of our previous work presented at ECCV last year (https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-20083-0_28), where we explored how a weighted subset of data can be as informative as the entire dataset for robust model training, our current study delves even deeper.

(2/n)

I am thrilled to announce that my latest research paper entitled "Adversarial Coreset Selection for Efficient Robust Training" has been accepted to the prestigious International Journal of Computer Vision (IJCV, Impact Factor=19.5).

(1/n)

Unless we accept ALL the papers that are technically correct, this would happen again and again. Because NOVELTY is subjective. I promise you in a couple of years the average review score would still drop.

A direct result of the current system! Reviewers have conflict of interest! An "expert" reviewer in an area is someone who has a similar paper in the same area under review. So, they feel that they need to score all the papers under their hand low to get in. https://x.com/SebastienBubeck/status/1686879727884304386
Fun fact 2: none of the papers discussed potential limitations! There was a time, not long ago, that most CV papers had a section dedicated to this.
Just finished the overwhelming eight-paper review assignment for #ICCV2023. Fun fact: all eight papers were claiming state-of-the-art results 🙂

Here's my submission as Digital Art for UniMelb GSA artprize. I used #stablediffusion to generate more than 1000 images (text2im, im2im, and inpainting) and then refined them in Photoshop. It was fun!

The piece is called "resilient mothers, creators of bright future."

If you happen to be at the conference virtually, I'd be more than happy to talk more about my work with you. :)

7/n, n=7.

#coreset_selection #adversarial_training #robust_learning #efficient_training #machine_learning #computer_vision #eccv22

For more details, please check out the camera-ready version of our paper. We have also open-sourced the implementation of our method on GitHub.

Camera-ready version: https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.00378
PyTorch code: https://github.com/hmdolatabadi/ACS

6/n

We show that using ACS could decrease the training time by 2-3 folds while almost preserving the same natural and robust accuracy. The main advantage of our approach is that it requires minimal change to be applied to arbitrary adversarial training objectives.

5/n

As a result, we can improve the efficiency of any arbitrary adversarial training objective in a unified, systematic way. To perform this, we introduce "adversarial coreset selection" (ACS).

4/n

Our #ECCV2022 paper shows that a weighted subset of the training data can be as informative as the entire data population. This way, we would benefit from running costly adversarial training just over a subset of the data.

3/n

We expose our neural networks to adversarial examples during training to defend against the threat of adversarial attacks. Unfortunately, this is costly and time-consuming as we must build adversarial examples for the entire training samples in each epoch.

2/n

In our recently accepted work to #ECCV2022 we ask: "Do we really need the entire training data for robust training against adversarial attacks?"

1/n

While it's nice to give best reviewer awards, IMO it's more important to let those whose reviews are bad know what is wrong. Negative feedback is a quicker way of resolving the review crisis at major ML conferences. Because anyone thinks they are a good reviewer.

#NeurIPS2021

As someone who's done his pre-PhD studies in signal processing, I am really impressed and happy to see those beautiful theories being applied to modern deep learning. It feels like reading Oppenheim's books all over again 😍

Setting its marvelous quality of image generation aside, I personally like reading the approach of StyleGAN3 which is based on basic signal processing including shannon-nyquist theorem and aliasing. https://x.com/hardmaru/status/1447735092630069253

@mpd37 @BlackHC @iclr_conf This needs more thinking, but here's my initial thought. This NeurIPS I spent on average 7 hours to review, while I had reviewers who barely spend 2 hours on my submission.

(3/3)

@mpd37 @BlackHC @iclr_conf Then, at the next conference, assign those high quality reviwers with priority to submissions where the authors are among high quality reviewers themselves. This way, you reward academics for high quality reviews: if they submit one, they would definitely get one.

(2/n)
Excited to share that I officially started my work as an #AmazonIntern today. Looking forward to collaborating with my amazing peers.
This is an important note when it comes to creating an #equal_opportunity for everyone in #academia and #machine_learning.

Virtual conferences are great, but they are mostly aligned with PDT time zone, especially their poster sessions. Personally, I would miss almost 50% of the poster sessions at every virtual conference. Perhaps it would be a better idea to allocate two poster sessions per paper.
Is anyone else annoyed by this Google scholar feature that when you open a person's profile, right-click on their papers to open them in a new tab it only opens a "about:blank#blocked" web-page?!

Wow! Saw a theoretical work with more than 50 pages including complicated proofs on arXiv that apparently has been submitted to the NeurIPS! How on earth can the reviewers go over such a paper in a short amount of time? Can they even look at the proofs closely?
Now, you can see the difference, right? ;) Even the colored version looks a lot better! Hope this help us remember and be considerate of each other. (9/n) (n=9)
Finally, don't forget to add grids! (8/n)
If there is an order to your plots, further improve readability by adjusting the order of your legends with the order of your plots ;) (7/n)
Now, it's time to make the axis ticks larger. Also, do not forget to add the scale/unit to the label of your axes! (6/n)
Enlarging the line width and marker size would make things look nicer! (5/n)
Then, we can also add different markers! (4/n)
Well, let's start easy: just add different line styles :) (3/n)

It is easily distinguishable if you can see the colors, but not without them. Inspired by the fact that many still prefer to put these sorts of figures in their papers, here is a thread to improve the readability of your plots. (2/n)

Can you easily distinguish the curves shown in the following figure?
This is how our paper figures would look like to a colorblind person! (1/n)

I am excited to be presenting our work at the #NeurIPS2020 pre-conference in Australia. My talk is this Saturday at 10 AM AEDT (GMT+11). The event is happening online, and you can find the full program details/link here: http://vision-learning.xyz/neurips2020/

Hope to see you around!

You can read more about our approach in the blog post. Please do reach out if you have any questions.

6/6

This way, we can construct adversarial examples that closely follow the clean data distribution. As such, AdvFlows can fool adversarial example detectors that assume adversaries come from a different distribution than the clean data.

5/6

We then adjust this transformation such that the overall density generates samples that are adversarial to a black-box DNN classifier.

4/6

In this work, we exploit flow-based models trained on clean data to construct local adversarial distributions around a target image. To this end, we perturb the base random vector by a tiny affine transformation.

3/6

Been working on denoising diffusion models of @hojonathanho et al. for image gen. Also, I wanted to give @PyTorchLightnin a go, so I decided to mix things up and train my very own DDP models using Lightning. Hope you enjoy as much as I did:

And yes, I know that there are some who get punished unfairly... but right now you see that EVERYONE is sad, so odds are most of them are exactly the type of reviewers others are complaining about. Because if ALL of us do our job correctly, then there's no room to complain.

2/2

What is interesting about peer-review is that people act to the reviews they get as if some extraterrestrial alien reviewed their work.

IMHO, the problem lies within the community itself. When you EXPECT fair reviews, you MUST do fair reviews.

1/2
We should treat "papers without code" as if they don't really exist, no matter how well-known their authors and/or venues are.

Just finished watching @andrewgwils's #ICML2020 tutorial on "Bayesian Deep Learning". I have to say it's quite good, with the right amount of details for newcomers like me. Highly recommend if you haven't watched it yet.

I feel an increase in the number of presentations where the speakers read their talk from a pre-written script. I guess this is due to everything being remote.

Personally, I prefer those talks that the presenter tries to speak naturally, even if they make mistakes.

#ICML2020

The @MLSS_Tuebingen lesson of the the day by the amazing @shakir_za on avoiding the common "Neutrality Traps":

"...once in a while try to find 'the person' who completely thinks the opposite of you, and [respectfully] seek criticism on your work..." 🧐

#MLSS2020

Inspiring advice by @MihaelaVDS on finding area of interest:
